title: [ROCm] Fix hipsparselt build integration and add TN mode support
body:
## Summary
- Fix hipsparselt (cuSPARSELt) build integration for ROCm (CMake target linking, include paths)
- Add CUSPARSELT_FORCE_TN env var to select TN (transpose-A) sparse matmul kernels on AMD GPUs, which are significantly faster on MI300X

## Test plan
- Benchmarked on MI300X with ROCm 7.2, hipsparselt 0.2
- TN mode shows 1.17-1.46x sparse-over-dense speedup vs 0.87-1.41x for NN across BERT and fixed-shape workloads

title: [TTD] Make ROCm build respect job-filter on PRs
body:
Stack from [ghstack](https://github.com/ezyang/ghstack/tree/0.14.0) (oldest at bottom):
* __->__ #174529
* #174528
* #174526
* #174525
* #174524

The ROCm build had a pre-existing `github.event_name == 'pull_request'`
condition that bypassed the job filter, causing it to always run on
every PR regardless of changed files. Switch to the standard job-filter
pattern so ROCm only runs when C++/CUDA/ROCm/build files are changed.

The original condition existed to avoid running the build twice on main
(push events). The standard pattern handles this correctly: on push
events, job-filter returns empty (run all), and on PRs, determine_jobs
controls which jobs run.

cc @jeffdaily @sunway513 @jithunnair-amd @pruthvistony @ROCmSupport @jataylo @hongxiayang @naromero77amd @pragupta @jerrymannil @xinyazhang

title: Plumb precompile metadata through async_compile to subprocess workers
body:
Stack from [ghstack](https://github.com/ezyang/ghstack) (oldest at bottom):
* #182330
* __->__ #182329
* #181267

The scheduling layers (cutedsl_scheduling, nv_universal_gemm_scheduling)
now extract input shapes, dtypes, and device index from IR nodes and
pass this as precompile_metadata through async_compile to the subprocess
worker. The worker calls the _precompile entry point generated in the
previous commit, triggering real MLIR→PTX→CUBIN compilation in the
subprocess rather than deferring it to first kernel invocation.

_build_precompile_metadata gracefully returns None for symbolic sizes so
the subprocess path degrades to source-only caching.

Authored with Claude.

cc @voznesenskym @penguinwu @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @ipiszy @kadeng @muchulee8 @amjames @chauhang @aakhundov @coconutruben @jataylo

title: Un-xfail test_sdpa_rewriter_14_cpu on ARM64 SVE256
body:
## Summary

Remove `xfailIf(IS_ARM64 and IS_CPU_CAPABILITY_SVE256)` from `test_sdpa_rewriter_14_cpu` in `test/inductor/test_fused_attention.py`.

The new OSDC aarch64 runner (`mt-l-arm64g3-16-62`, enabled trunk-wide via #183482) reports `IS_CPU_CAPABILITY_SVE256=True` but does not reproduce the numerical mismatch from #177244. The decorator now flips passing runs to `FAILED CONSISTENTLY` via "Unexpected success" whenever target determination pulls the file into the shard.

Failure first surfaced at `07734446eb` on `trunk / linux-jammy-aarch64-py3.10 / test-osdc (default, 4, 5, mt-l-arm64g3-16-62)` and has been consistent across every subsequent main commit whose TD scope selected `test_fused_attention.py`.

Closes #177244 (if the issue still applies to the older `m7g.4xlarge` runner family, we can re-narrow the gate later).

## Test plan

- [ ] `pull / trunk` aarch64 OSDC test-osdc shard 4/5 turns green on this branch.
- [ ] No regressions on x86 or other aarch64 shards.

title: POC flex gemm
body: ## Add GEMM epilogue fusion prototype This PR prototypes a GEMM epilogue fusion higher-order op plus Inductor lowering/codegen paths for fusing pointwise epilogues into GEMM-family ops. ### What is in this PR - Adds `gemm_epilogue_fusion` and convenience wrappers for `mm`, `addmm`, `bmm`, `baddbmm`, grouped-mm, scaled-mm, and GEMM-shaped `matmul` epilogue fusion. - Adds Dynamo/ProxyTensor/Inductor plumbing so the epilogue callable can be traced and lowered with the GEMM op. - Adds TRITON coverage for common GEMM epilogue paths, including `_scaled_mm + epilogue`. - Adds QUACK backend routing/codegen for `mm`, `addmm`, `bmm`, `baddbmm`, and MXFP8 blockscaled `_scaled_mm` epilogues. - Adds CUTLASS/CUTEDSL backend acceptance/routing hooks; CUTLASS runtime validation still needs a proper CUDA/CUTLASS environment. - Adds targeted tests in `test/inductor/test_gemm_epilogue_fusion.py`. ### API note The generic form now supports passing the user-facing shorthand callable directly: ```py gemm_epilogue_fusion(torch.mm, (x, y), epilogue_fn, kernel_options={"backend": backend}) ``` The experiment below intentionally uses `torch.mm` instead of `torch.ops.aten.mm.default` to exercise that support. ### Current limitations / follow-ups - QUACK generic epilogue bridge currently supports pointwise epilogues, not reductions such as `acc / acc.sum(dim=1, keepdim=True)`. - QUACK `_scaled_mm` currently handles the MXFP8 blockscaled path; tensorwise float32 scales, bias, `scale_result`, and `use_fast_accum=True` are not wired yet. - `matmul_epilogue` is limited to 2D `mm` and 3D `bmm` shaped inputs. - `torch.nn.functional.linear` convenience wrapping is intentionally left out for now. QuACK companion work: https://github.com/drisspg/quack/pull/new/make-epilogue-generic ### Repro / codegen experiment ```py #!/usr/bin/env python3 import re from pathlib import Path from typing import Annotated import torch import torch._inductor.config as inductor_config import typer from torch._higher_order_ops import gemm_epilogue_fusion from torch._inductor.utils import run_and_get_code from transformer_nuggets.utils.benchmark import profiler DTYPES = {"float16": torch.float16, "bfloat16": torch.bfloat16, "float32": torch.float32} EPILOGUES = { "identity": lambda acc: acc, "relu": lambda acc: acc.relu(), "affine-relu": lambda acc: (acc * 0.5 + 1.0).relu(), "gelu": lambda acc: torch.nn.functional.gelu(acc), "silu": lambda acc: torch.nn.functional.silu(acc), "sigmoid": lambda acc: torch.sigmoid(acc), "tanh": lambda acc: torch.tanh(acc), "exp": lambda acc: torch.exp(acc), "abs": lambda acc: torch.abs(acc), "square": lambda acc: acc * acc, "clamp": lambda acc: acc.clamp(min=0.0, max=6.0), "leaky-relu": lambda acc: torch.nn.functional.leaky_relu(acc, 0.01), "where-leaky-relu": lambda acc: torch.where(acc > 0, acc, acc * 0.01), "sqrt-abs": lambda acc: torch.sqrt(torch.abs(acc) + 1.0), "rsqrt-abs": lambda acc: torch.rsqrt(torch.abs(acc) + 1.0), "normalize-sum-n": lambda acc: acc / acc.sum(dim=1, keepdim=True), } def write_text(path: Path, text: str) -> None: path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True) path.write_text(text) def main( out_dir: Annotated[ Path, typer.Option(help="Directory for exported graph and generated code artifacts."), ] = Path(__file__).resolve().parent / "gemm_epilogue_codegen", m: Annotated[int, typer.Option(help="Rows of A/output.")] = 2048, n: Annotated[int, typer.Option(help="Columns of B/output.")] = 2048, k: Annotated[int, typer.Option(help="Reduction dimension.")] = 2048, dtype: Annotated[ str, typer.Option(help="Input dtype.", case_sensitive=False), ] = "float16", epilogue: Annotated[ str, typer.Option(help="Epilogue to try; see EPILOGUES in this script."), ] = "relu", backend: Annotated[ str, typer.Option(help="Requested GEMM epilogue backend: TRITON, CUTEDSL, or QUACK."), ] = "QUACK", profile: Annotated[bool, typer.Option(help="Whether to run the compiled code with a profiler.")] = False, ) -> None: if not torch.cuda.is_available(): raise RuntimeError("This experiment expects CUDA to be available") if dtype not in DTYPES: raise typer.BadParameter(f"dtype must be one of {', '.join(DTYPES)}") if epilogue not in EPILOGUES: raise typer.BadParameter(f"epilogue must be one of {', '.join(EPILOGUES)}") torch.manual_seed(0) a = torch.randn(m, k, device="cuda", dtype=DTYPES[dtype]) b = torch.randn(k, n, device="cuda", dtype=DTYPES[dtype]) epilogue_fn = EPILOGUES[epilogue] def fn(x, y): return gemm_epilogue_fusion( torch.mm, (x, y), epilogue_fn, kernel_options={"backend": backend}, ) out_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True) eager = epilogue_fn(torch.mm(a, b)) compiled = torch.compile(fn, backend="inductor", fullgraph=True) with inductor_config.patch({"max_autotune_gemm": False}): actual, codes = run_and_get_code(compiled, a, b) torch.testing.assert_close(actual, eager) kernels = [] for i, code in enumerate(codes): write_text(out_dir / f"inductor_output_{i}.py", code) kernels.extend( re.findall( r"async_compile\.triton\([^\n]+, '''\n(.*?)\n'''", code, re.DOTALL, ) ) for i, kernel in enumerate(kernels): write_text(out_dir / f"triton_kernel_{i}.py", kernel) summary = [ "FlexJam GEMM epilogue fusion experiment", f"torch: {torch.__file__}", f"git_version: {torch.version.git_version}", f"shape: ({m}, {k}) x ({k}, {n})", f"dtype: {dtype}", f"epilogue: {epilogue}", f"backend: {backend}", f"inductor_outputs: {len(codes)}", ] summary.extend(f" - {out_dir / f'inductor_output_{i}.py'}" for i in range(len(codes))) summary.append(f"triton_kernels: {len(kernels)}") summary.extend(f" - {out_dir / f'triton_kernel_{i}.py'}" for i in range(len(kernels))) summary.append(f"max_abs_diff: {(actual - eager).abs().max().item()}") write_text(out_dir / "summary.txt", "\n".join(summary) + "\n") print("\n".join(summary)) if profile: print("\nProfiling the compiled code...") with profiler(f"gemm_epilogue_{epilogue}_backend_{backend}") as prof: compiled(a, b) if __name__ == "__main__": typer.run(main) ``` Some profilign to make sure it is working for relu

title: Add TORCH_CHECK_INDEX in convert_indices_from_coo_to_csr_cpu
body:
The `to_sparse_csr` CPU implementation `convert_indices_from_coo_to_csr_cpu`  doesn't validate the COO indices, which may lead to illegal memory access.

This PR fixes that by adding checks on the index before accessing the data.

```Python
# repro
num_nonzeros = 2048

#row,col
dense_size = (1024, 256)


row_indices = torch.randint(0, dense_size[0], (num_nonzeros,))
col_indices = torch.randint(0, dense_size[1], (num_nonzeros,))
coo_indices = torch.stack((row_indices, col_indices))

# this should work
sparse_coo = torch.sparse_coo_tensor(
    coo_indices,
    torch.ones(coo_indices.size(1), dtype=bool),
    dense_size,
)
dst_node_indices = sparse_coo.to_sparse_csr()
print(f"Works well {dst_node_indices}")


# altering row_indices, now it's expected to fail

row_indices[-1] = dense_size[0] + 42

coo_indices = torch.stack((row_indices, col_indices))

# this should not work
sparse_coo = torch.sparse_coo_tensor(
    coo_indices,
    torch.ones(coo_indices.size(1), dtype=bool),
    dense_size,
)
dst_node_indices = sparse_coo.to_sparse_csr()
print("Should never reach here")
```

title: [CD] Refactor Windows wheel build scripts (Python pipeline)
body:
## Summary

Port the Windows wheel CD build scripts off the legacy `.bat` chain onto a Python pipeline, mirroring the Linux split landed in #182409.

The new entry chain on x86_64 wheel builds:

```
binary_windows_build.sh
  -> windows/internal/vc_install_helper.bat   (ensure VS2022 BuildTools)
  -> windows/set_desired_python.sh            (install Python.org Python)
  -> python windows/build_env_setup.py        (vcvarsall + CUDA/XPU env + MAGMA + sccache)
  -> python windows/build_install_deps.py     (pip deps + libuv)
  -> python windows/build_wheel.py            (python -m build --wheel)
```

vcvarsall env capture mirrors `torch.utils.cpp_extension._jit._get_vc_env` from #180707 (vswhere discovery, `cmd /u /c` for UTF-16LE output, diff against current env so additions layer on top).

`internal/install_python.bat`, `cuda_install.bat`, `xpu_install.bat`, and `vc_install_helper.bat` are kept as Python-invoked installers -- same pragmatic precedent as the Linux refactor (`install_cuda.sh` etc.). Heavy installer logic stays in bash/cmd; orchestration moves to Python.

**Out of scope** for this PR (intentionally, mirroring #182409's scoping discipline):
- `windows-arm64` wheels (separate `arm64/build_pytorch.bat` chain, untouched)
- `libtorch` zip artifacts (still routes through legacy `internal/build_wheels.bat`)
- `cpu-s390x` (Linux only anyway)
- Windows pull-CI build (uses a separate `win-test-helpers/build_pytorch.bat`, untouched)

Files removed: `windows/{build_pytorch,setup_build,cpu,cuda,xpu}.bat` + `windows/internal/{build_wheels,init,setup,check_opts,check_nvtx,check_deps,cuda_config,clone,clean}.bat`.

## Test plan

- [ ] x86_64 cpu wheel build (Win10/Win11 runner)
- [ ] x86_64 cuda wheel build for each supported CUDA version (12.6, 12.8, 12.9, 13.0, 13.2)
- [ ] x86_64 xpu wheel build
- [ ] Confirm windows-arm64 wheel + libtorch builds are unaffected
- [ ] Confirm pull-CI Windows (win-build.sh -> win-test-helpers/build_pytorch.bat) is unaffected

Authored with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code).

title: Use std::in_range for integer range checking
body:
C++20 provides [`std::in_range`](https://en.cppreference.com/w/cpp/utility/in_range.html) which allows checking
if an integer value can fit in a given integer type. This PR
replaces such checks with in_range.

std::in_range is safer than direct comparisons
because it avoids implicit casts.

The call sites to update were obtained by grepping
for `"numeric_limits.*min"`.

PyTorch supports C++20 now #176662

I have also proposed a clang-tidy checker to take
care of this automatically going forward https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/issues/190665

title: Run clang-tidy with readability-container-contains
body:
C++20 provides a `contains` member function for all associative containers.
Prior to C++20, the common pattern to check if an element is a member of a
container was to either `container.find(key) != container.end()` or
`container.count(key) > 0`. This PR replaces those instances with `contains`.

All the changes were done by running clang-tidy with the
`readability-container-contains` check enabled.

PyTorch now supports C++20 #176662

title: [ShapesSpec] Support args, *args, **kwargs at the spec / dynamo source level
body:
Stack from [ghstack](https://github.com/ezyang/ghstack/tree/0.15.0) (oldest at bottom):
* #184429
* __->__ #184129
* #184299
* #184271
* #182764

Adds *args / **kwargs support to the ShapesSpec API:

- `ParamsSpec` now takes a single dict keyed by parameter name with
  two reserved sentinel keys for the variadic slots:

      ParamsSpec({
          "x":        TensorSpec([ShapeVar("batch"), None]),
          "*args":    [TensorSpec([ShapeVar("a")]), None],
          "**kwargs": {"foo": TensorSpec([ShapeVar("b")])},
      })

  Regular keys are leaf specs; `"*args"` takes a positional list
  (heterogeneous per index, positions past the end are static);
  `"**kwargs"` takes a dict by kwarg name. The sentinel keys are
  literal regardless of the function's actual `*args`/`**kwargs`
  Python names, so a spec is portable across renames.

- `LocalSource` gains two flags, `is_varargs` and `is_varkw`,
  populated at frame entry from `co_flags & CO_VARARGS` /
  `CO_VARKEYWORDS`. These let the spec resolver distinguish *args
  / **kwargs from regular list-/dict-typed inputs (whose
  GetItemSource/DictGetItemSource shapes are structurally identical
  at the dynamo level).

- `lookup_spec_from_dynamo_source` now handles three patterns:
  named arg, indexed *args access, and keyed **kwargs access.
  Returns the matching leaf spec or `None` (= static).

- `ParamsSpec.__repr__` and `to_jsonable` now include the *args /
  **kwargs slots (previously dropped); `to_jsonable` round-trips
  to the constructor format.

This is the base on top of which validation (next commit) and export
plug-in (commit after) layer cleanly.


cc @voznesenskym @penguinwu @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @kadeng @chauhang @amjames @jataylo @azahed98

title: Guard pipeline schedule adjacency
body:
## Guard adjacent-stage communication in PipelineScheduleMulti via init-time validation

### Root Cause

`PipelineScheduleMulti` assumes communication only happens between adjacent stages (`stage_idx ± 1`), but this invariant was never enforced. Non-adjacent stage communication (e.g. skip-connection topologies) would proceed silently and produce incorrect results or cryptic runtime errors downstream.

### Implementation

Adds `_validate_adjacent_stage_communication()`, called once during `_initialize_stages` (first forward init or mode switch). It inspects `args_recv_info` for recv sources and `act_send_info` for send destinations, comparing against the expected adjacent-stage set. Raises a clear `RuntimeError` when non-adjacent peers are detected.

This approach was chosen over per-step validation to avoid any runtime overhead on the hot path.

### Tradeoffs & Limitations

- Only validates forward communication (recv sources + send destinations). Backward grad communication follows the same topology by construction via `_create_grad_send_info`.
- Runs at init-time only, not per-step. A topology that changes mid-execution (not currently possible) would not be caught.

### Testing Performed

- [x] Negative test: non-adjacent topology (skip connection) is rejected with clear error
- [x] Positive test: adjacent topology passes validation
- [x] Edge case test: middle stage with no recv info passes validation
- [x] Ran focused pytest locally against `test_schedule.py`

title: Fix FakeTensor device hint in DTensor sharding propagation
body:
Pass `device=arg.mesh.device_type` in `_rebuild_tensor_from_dtensor_meta` so FakeTensors carry the correct device type instead of defaulting to CPU. This fixes ops like `histc` whose meta registration rejects certain dtypes on CPU.

Also guard memory tracking to skip DTensor sharding propagation ops. DTensor propagation may use a nested `FakeTensorMode` to compute output metadata (shapes, placements). The real memory usage comes from the local op on shard data, which runs outside DTensor's fake mode — so propagation ops should never be tracked.

The guard saves the `FakeTensorMode` instance active at tracker entry (`None` in eager, a `FakeTensorMode` in SAC) and only tracks ops when `active_fake_mode() is self._fake_mode_on_entry`. Since DTensor propagation creates a different nested `FakeTensorMode`, its ops are skipped in both contexts:
- Eager (entry mode is `None`): real ops have no fake mode (`None is None`), DTensor propagation ops are skipped (`B is not None`).
- SAC (entry mode is `A`): SAC ops run under mode `A` (`A is A`), DTensor propagation ops are skipped (`B is not A`).

Authored by Claude.

cc @gujinghui @PenghuiCheng @XiaobingSuper @jianyuh @jgong5 @mingfeima @sanchitintel @ashokei @jingxu10 @min-jean-cho @yanbing-j @Guobing-Chen @Xia-Weiwen @snadampal

body:
Continues the PR https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/170646
Address issue https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/167885

Address most of the points noted in https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/170646:

- Added an API to query the mempool ID and check if two CUDA graphs share the same pool. If so, using the outputs from the first CUDA graph in the second graph is safe.
- Handled pinned host memory tensors for the copy ops. 
- Filters out the tensors that don't create any CUDA work.

TODO:

title: [inductor][overlap] Add simple_overlap scheduler pass for comm/compute overlap
body:
Stack from [ghstack](https://github.com/ezyang/ghstack/tree/0.15.0) (oldest at bottom):
* #184240
* __->__ #184235

Summary:

Adds `simple_overlap`, a new inductor scheduler pass that overlaps
NCCL collectives with compute. Moves collective starts earlier and
waits later via adjacent swaps with O(1) incremental memory checks.

Designed for safe default use -- no collective reordering, no memory
regression, no runtime estimation heuristics.

## Motivation

Existing comm/compute overlap in graph_trainer relies on
`transformer_block_bucketing` (TBB), a graph-level pass that
requires model-specific knowledge of transformer layer boundaries.
This pass operates at the inductor scheduler level -- it works on
any model with NCCL collectives, after fusion and peak-memory
reordering, using the same memory infrastructure as the existing
iterative overlap passes.

## Algorithm

For each collective: swap backward one position at a time toward
its last data dependency. Stop at another collective (preserving
NCCL ordering) or if the swap would increase peak memory.

For each wait: swap forward one position at a time toward its
first consumer. Stop at another wait or if the swap would increase
peak memory.

Memory is tracked incrementally using `_initialize_memory_tracking`
(same as `reorder_communication_preserving_peak_memory`), so each
swap is O(1) instead of O(N).


● ## Local 8-GPU H100 sweep — Llama 3.1 8B, graph_trainer, bs=2, steps 10-35 avg

  ### TP=1 FSDP=8

  | Config | Description | Step (ms) | MFU (%) | Mem (GiB) | vs A | SO diff |
  |--------|-------------|-----------|---------|-----------|------|---------|
  | A | TBB manual overlap | 2061 | 46.58 | 79.93 | — | — |
  | E | TBB + simple_overlap | 2062 | 46.55 | 79.93 | +0.0% | A→E: +0.0% |
  | C | overlap_scheduling | 2083 | 46.08 | 78.60 | +1.1% | — |
  | D | overlap_scheduling + simple_overlap | 2088 | 45.97 | 78.60 | +1.3% | C→D: +0.2% |
  | B | 500MB bucket + simple_overlap | 2081 | 46.13 | 78.60 | +1.0% | — |

  ### TP=8

  | Config | Description | Step (ms) | MFU (%) | Mem (GiB) | vs A | SO diff |
  |--------|-------------|-----------|---------|-----------|------|---------|
  | A | TBB manual overlap | 470 | 25.54 | 20.05 | — | — |
  | E | TBB + simple_overlap | 469 | 25.59 | 20.05 | -0.2% | A→E: -0.2% |
  | C | overlap_scheduling | 469 | 25.58 | 20.05 | -0.2% | — |
  | D | overlap_scheduling + simple_overlap | 470 | 25.54 | 20.05 | +0.0% | C→D: +0.2% |
  | B | 500MB bucket + simple_overlap | 468 | 25.63 | 20.05 | -0.4% | — |

  Machine: devgpu034 (8x H100), CUDA 12.8, PyTorch @ `9d287052101` (main + simple_overlap stack)

  SO diff compares paired configs with/without simple_overlap enabled:
  - A→E (TBB ± SO): neutral at both TP=1 and TP=8
  - C→D (overlap_scheduling ± SO): +0.2% both cases (within noise)


## API

```python
torch._inductor.config.aten_distributed_optimizations.enable_simple_overlap = True
```

Works with existing `bucket_all_gathers_fx` / `bucket_reduce_scatters_fx`
for collective bucketing before overlap scheduling.

Authored by Claude.

Test Plan:
Tested on 8xH100 with graph_trainer llama3 8B across FSDP,
TP2+FSDP4, and TP4+FSDP2 configurations.

title: [Profiler] Expose ITraceActivity to Python for direct chrome trace ex…
body:
…port

Expose Kineto's ITraceActivity objects to Python via pybind, enabling Python-side chrome trace export that bypasses Kineto's C++ serialization, and implement fast gz synchronization.

Replace export_chrome_trace and tensorboard trace handler with a Python streaming exporter that writes events
  directly to disk through an optional gzip writer (commit 2).
  
Why: The old path serialized the full JSON in C++, wrote a temp file, then re-read and gzip-compressed it. The new path streams per-event, so .json.gz goes from
  5.8s to 3.2s (1.8x) on 300K events. Plain .json is slower (1.75s vs 0.87s) but acceptable since .gz is the production path.

  Benchmark (GB200, 300K events, 50K mm+add iterations):
```
  ┌──────────┬───────┬───────┬─────────┐
  │   Path   │  Old  │  New  │ Speedup │
  ├──────────┼───────┼───────┼─────────┤
  │ .json    │ 0.87s │ 1.75s │ 0.5x    │
  ├──────────┼───────┼───────┼─────────┤
  │ .json.gz │ 5.77s │ 3.15s │ 1.8x    │
  └──────────┴───────┴───────┴─────────┘
```

More importantly, because Python controls the write loop, per-event transforms (e.g. CUDA graph kernel annotation injection, stream reassignment) can be applied inline during export rather than requiring a separate load-modify-save post-processing pass.

The C++ changes are minimal:
- Forward-declare and pybind-wrap ITraceActivity with key accessors (name, timestamp, duration, deviceId, resourceId, correlationId, flowId, metadataJson, linkedActivity correlation)
- Add ProfilerResult::traceActivities() that forwards to the existing ActivityTraceInterface::activities() vector

Also adds TestMetadataJsonFormat tests that guard the format of ITraceActivity::metadataJson() for kernel events, since the Python export path splices these strings verbatim into the output JSON and extracts fields like graph_node_id via string matching.

Adds tests to verify that old and new export paths produce identical traces. 




Authored by Claude.

body:
Fixes: #183988 ```python In [1]: import torch ...: ...: def fn(x): ...: idx = torch.randperm(x.shape[0], device=x.device) ...: return x[idx] ...: ...: x = torch.randn(8, 64, device="cuda") ...: ...: # Eager: OK ...: print(fn(x).shape) ...: ...: # aot_eager: OK ...: torch._dynamo.reset() ...: print(torch.compile(fn, backend="aot_eager")(x).shape) # torch.Size([8, 64]) ...: ...: # Inductor: WORKS FINE ...: torch._dynamo.reset() ...: print(torch.compile(fn, backend="inductor")(x).shape) torch.Size([8, 64]) torch.Size([8, 64]) torch.Size([8, 64]) In [2]: ``` I checked Inductor traces to verify whether the optimized kernel is being generated. The `test_randperm_index_slice_range` does not generate the optimized kernel. Should I add another entry in misc_patterns.py for that case, or is that not necessary? Thanks!

title: [DDE] Fix data-dependent errors in pooling meta functions
body:
Stack from [ghstack](https://github.com/ezyang/ghstack/tree/0.15.0) (oldest at bottom):
* #183814
* __->__ #183774
* #183754

Fix GuardOnDataDependentSymNode in pooling output shape computation:

- Simplify div_rtn(x, y) to x // y. The remainder adjustment for
  round-toward-negative-infinity is redundant in Python where // already
  floors. The old code guarded on `r != 0 and bool(r < 0) != bool(y < 0)`
  which triggers DDE on unbacked SymInts.

- Replace compound `torch._check(a and b)` with `torch._check(sym_and(a, b))`
  in pool2d_shape_check and pool3d_shape_check. Python `and` on SymBools
  forces bool conversion, triggering DDE. sym_and avoids this.

Fixes DDE for avg_pool2d, max_pool2d, avg_pool3d, max_pool3d, avg_pool1d
when spatial dimensions are unbacked SymInts with stride > 1.

Authored by Claude. Vetted and reviewed by @laithsakka
